_doc: Each entry is a published industrial standard or seminal academic source
the platform commits to honouring. Every Tier-E formula MUST cite a
standard_id in this file; every Tier-B AI prompt that mentions a metric
MUST cite the same standard_id as the formula it references; every
Tier-D chip on a dashboard MUST display the standard's short name.
This is the layer that catches the OEE-class bug: a formula's
implementation must SUPERSET the standard's required_inputs, OR the
formula must explicitly declare itself a `partial_variant` with a
full_title         long name of the publication
publisher          ISO / SAE / SMRP / Nakajima / etc.
edition_year       publication year (so drift can be detected)
clauses            map of clause -> { required_inputs, formula, units }

standards.full_title: Petroleum, petrochemical and natural gas industries — Collection and exchange of reliability and maintenance data for equipment

9.2_availability.name: Availability
9.2_availability.notes: Inherent availability per ISO 14224 §9.2; excludes administrative + logistic delays. For operational availability, see §9.2 alternate formula.

9.3_mtbf.name: MTBF — Mean Time Between Failures
9.3_mtbf.notes: ISO 14224 §9.3 specifies OPERATING time between failures (excludes idle / planned downtime). Calendar-time approximations should declare themselves partial.

9.4_mttr.name: MTTR — Mean Time To Repair
9.4_mttr.notes: ISO 14224 §9.4 distinguishes active repair time (technician on the asset) from logistic/admin time. Implementations using total downtime should declare themselves a partial variant or use MTTR_total.

9.5_failure_modes.name: Failure Mode Taxonomy
9.5_failure_modes.notes: ISO 14224:2016 Table B.4 defines the failure mode taxonomy used for cross-asset comparison.

standards.full_title: Automation systems and integration — Key performance indicators (KPIs) for manufacturing operations management — Part 2: Definitions and descriptions

5.5_oee.name: OEE — Overall Equipment Effectiveness
5.5_oee.notes: Nakajima TPM (1988) defined the three-factor form; ISO 22400-2:2014 §5.5 formalised the standard. Any implementation missing one factor MUST declare itself a partial variant.

5.5_availability_factor.name: Availability (OEE factor)
5.5_availability_factor.canonical_formula: Availability = (Planned Production Time - Downtime) / Planned Production Time
5.5_availability_factor.notes: ISO 22400-2 availability uses PLANNED production time as the denominator (different from ISO 14224 §9.2 inherent availability).

5.5_performance_factor.name: Performance (OEE factor)
5.5_performance_factor.canonical_formula: Performance = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Count) / Run Time
5.5_performance_factor.notes: Requires per-asset ideal cycle time (planned rate). Without this fuel field, OEE drops to partial (Availability × Quality).

5.5_quality_factor.name: Quality (OEE factor)
5.5_quality_factor.notes: Defective units / rework / scrap reduce quality.

standards.full_title: Introduction to TPM: Total Productive Maintenance — Seiichi Nakajima

six_big_losses.name: Six Big Losses
six_big_losses.notes: Breakdowns, setup/adjustment, idling/minor stops, reduced speed, startup defects, scrap/rework. Maps to OEE: first 2 → Availability, next 2 → Performance, last 2 → Quality.

oee_three_factor.name: OEE = A × P × Q (original definition)
oee_three_factor.notes: World-class benchmark: 85% (Nakajima 1988). Identical formula to ISO 22400-2 §5.5 but predates it.

standards.full_title: Evaluation Criteria for Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) Processes

5.4_consequence_buckets.name: RCM Consequence Classification
5.4_consequence_buckets.notes: SAE JA1011 §5.4 requires every failure mode be classified into one of four consequence categories. Drives PM strategy selection.

6_pm_interval.name: PM Interval Selection
6_pm_interval.notes: P-F interval (potential-to-functional failure) divided by safety factor; SAE JA1011 §6 + MIL-STD-2173.

standards.full_title: Society for Maintenance & Reliability Professionals — Body of Knowledge Best Practices, 5th Edition

2.1.1_pm_compliance.name: PM Compliance Rate
2.1.1_pm_compliance.canonical_formula: PM Compliance = PMs Completed On Schedule / PMs Scheduled
2.1.1_pm_compliance.notes: SMRP Metric 2.1.1. World-class target: >= 90%. Period-scoped (typically 30d / 90d / 365d). The 30-day threshold for is_due is platform convention, not SMRP.

4.2_parts_management.name: Parts Inventory Turnover
4.2_parts_management.canonical_formula: Inventory Turnover = Annual Parts Consumption Value / Average Inventory Value
4.2_parts_management.notes: SMRP 4.2; world-class >= 2 turns/year for critical spares.

standards.full_title: Failure modes and effects analysis (FMEA and FMECA)

rpn.name: Risk Priority Number (RPN)
rpn.notes: Classical FMEA RPN per IEC 60812:2018. Newer guidance (AIAG-VDA 2019) favours Action Priority over RPN, but RPN remains widely used.

standards.full_title: Rolling bearings — Dynamic load ratings and rating life

5_basic_rating_life.name: L10 Basic Rating Life
5_basic_rating_life.notes: ISO 281 §5: p=3 for ball bearings, p=10/3 for roller bearings. L10 = 90% survival probability. For L_n at other reliabilities use a1 factor (Table 1).

5_l10_hours.name: L10h Bearing Life Hours
5_l10_hours.notes: Combined-chain formula: L10h derives the L10 revolutions inline from C, P, and the bearing-type exponent p, then converts to hours at constant rpm n. Implementations may either compute L10 first and pass it in, or use the combined form.

standards.full_title: Darcy-Weisbach equation for pressure loss in pipe flow

pressure_drop.name: Pipe Pressure Drop
pressure_drop.notes: Friction factor f from Moody chart or Colebrook-White equation; depends on Reynolds number + relative roughness.

standards.full_title: Centrifugal Pumps for Petroleum, Petrochemical and Natural Gas Industries

pump_head.name: Pump Total Head
pump_head.notes: API 610 §6.1. Pump total dynamic head; combines pressure, velocity, and elevation components per Bernoulli.

npsh_available.name: NPSH Available
npsh_available.notes: API 610 §9.1.3. NPSHA must exceed NPSHR + margin to avoid cavitation.

motor_power.name: Motor Output Power
motor_power.notes: NEMA MG-1 §12. Three-phase output power; multiply by 0.746 to convert hp→kW.

service_factor.name: Service Factor
service_factor.notes: NEMA MG-1 §14. Typical SF=1.15 for general-purpose; 1.0 for high-temp environments.

standards.full_title: Energy Standard for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings

cooling_load.name: Sensible Cooling Load
cooling_load.canonical_formula: Q_sensible = m_air × Cp × ΔT (or U×A×ΔT for envelope)
cooling_load.notes: ASHRAE 90.1 + Fundamentals Handbook. Building cooling load = envelope + internal + ventilation.

min_wall_thickness.name: Minimum Pipe Wall Thickness
min_wall_thickness.notes: ASME B31.3 §304.1.2. Minimum required pipe wall thickness for internal pressure design.

standards.full_title: PMI Project Management Body of Knowledge — Earned Value Management

spi.name: Schedule Performance Index (SPI)
spi.notes: PMBOK 7th ed. §3.5.7. SPI < 1 = behind schedule; SPI > 1 = ahead.

cpi.name: Cost Performance Index (CPI)
cpi.notes: PMBOK 7th ed. §3.5.7. CPI < 1 = over budget; CPI > 1 = under.

z_score.name: Z-Score
z_score.notes: Tukey / Pearson. 3-sigma threshold = 99.73% of normal distribution; |z|>3 indicates rare event. Platform sensor anomaly detector uses this.

standards.full_title: Platform-internal maturity model — 5-stair ladder per Hive Readiness rubric

stair_composite.name: Hive Stair Composite Score
stair_composite.canonical_formula: composite = avg(reliability_pct, operations_pct, safety_pct, people_pct, cost_pct) clamped 0-100
stair_composite.notes: Platform-internal rubric documented in compute_hive_readiness RPC. Maps composite → stair (0-5): 0-30=stair0, 31-50=stair1, 51-70=stair2, 71-85=stair3, 86-95=stair4, 96-100=stair5.

standards.full_title: Weibull++ methodology — Weibull distribution fitting for time-to-failure analysis

wearout_shape.name: Weibull Shape Parameter (β)
wearout_shape.canonical_formula: β > 1 indicates wear-out; β = 1 random; β < 1 infant mortality
wearout_shape.notes: Weibull (1951). Used by the platform's batch-risk-scoring to detect wear-out modes (β > 1.5 typically triggers a PM interval review).
